text: So what's your limiting belief around money? You don't have to put in the chat, but I encourage you to think about it. And if you're open to putting it in the chat, please do. A lot of tradies feel like they have to work really hard to deserve money, which is a big fallacy.

We don't have to work hard to earn good money. You only need to look at every YouTuber that exists to understand that we don't have to work hard to earn lots of money. Thank you for sharing, Daniel. I constantly look at the price I come up with and believe it's too much due to the industry.
Well, now that you're all answering, I'm losing it.

But lately I've been using the saying, you're better off going broke at home. It is so true. It constantly stuns me how many new clients come to me and realize that they've been undercharging to the point they may as well have sat home and not gone to work at all.

Makes me really sad to think that people are working so hard and not earning what they deserve. And I understand that for many of you, there's a transition to get to that point. Like we can't immediately put our prices up to where they need to be for many of you.

However, lots of you believe when you see that quoted figure on the page that feels like it's too much and nobody's going to pay it. And so we go and tweak it and we do ourselves out of profitability.
It happens even with some of our really advanced long-term tradiepreneurs. Funny, recently, as yesterday, I had a conversation with one of our tradiepreneurs that was doing this.

They were missing out on a percentage, got really frightened, decided that this year was the year they were just going to stick with it, and managed to get a job over the line that was double what the original allowance was. I do not know who Yom is, but let's find out. Ah, it's Tom, not Yum. Tom. Hello, Tom. Yum.

Um, let's have a look at a few more of these. Amy, that I need a huge safety net in order to make big money decisions. I'm so scared I will be left struggling to survive if I make a mistake. That's a big one, Amy.

Um, I think that as we age and we have families and our life circumstances age, our risk appetite decreases for all of us. We get to a point where we feel as though we don't have enough time to make back sufficiently to get us where we need to be.
And so I actually support that your risk appetite should change as you change, as your family changes, as your business changes, as your commitment changes.

So I don't actually think that that's such a bad thing, but what I would love you to do is get factual about it rather than Remembering that feelings aren't facts, get factual about, well, what would you need to survive? How long do you think you'd need to survive?
What that figure is, is the figure that you need in the bank.
Typically it's about 3 months. 3 months is a huge period of time. For anyone that was here when Woz first dipped out, that first 3 months, that was a massive period of time.

It's enormous to make all the sufficient changes necessary for you to either reinstate your business— that might look like getting a manager in might look like selling the business, might look like, um, I don't know, doing something with your mortgage if you needed to. There are so many opportunities available to you.
3 months is a huge buffer to give yourself, but your risk appetite might be different and you might decide you need 6 months, and that's okay. Everybody's different. Um, thank you for sharing.

Dan Stumped. Or maybe it would be fair to say, Dan, you've done a lot of work on this in the last particularly 12 months. So I'm not surprised that you stumped on this question. For Asher and Eloise, everything you said, but thinking we are getting better at charging our worth.

Yeah, it's— I think in a case like yours, it is really hard. Perhaps I should acknowledge it's very challenging, particularly for builders and landscapers that are doing longer-term projects, to feel comfortable with what they're charging.

I think it happens to all of us, but when we talk— and when we're talking about builders landscape is because the projects are so large, they've gone for such a period of time, the price is so large, it gets really uncomfortable, and you feel like this beautiful person that you've just met can't afford that, so I'm going to discount my prices so they can afford it.
I'd really like to do the project, I like this couple, I want to help them out, whatever it looks like.

When we learn that— again, when we take the feelings away and we looked at the facts and the facts alone, there is a profitability level that we have to have within the business and that's what we need to honor when we do our pricing.

And I think when you then structure that with the right kind of sales process to ensure that it supports a solution and an emotional connection, the pricing gets far less pushback. So the person I was talking about before is a builder.
They kept discounting themselves because they really wanted to win the work to help develop their reputation.

The problem with that was they're running at a really low margin, like most builders do, to be honest, because we don't factor in enough contingency But their margin was getting that low that it was meaning that they didn't really want to do it anymore. What's the point? They're not making enough money.
Why would we keep doing this? So they're priced appropriately. They use the hourly rate calculator.

They built in some contingencies. Um, we built in some, some office time buffers, all of that kind of stuff. And so the quote doubled from where the, the client had said, I had this amount of money to spend. It doubled.
So significant when we're talking about a build, um, and they still said yes because the process was there to make them emotionally connected and understand that they were delivering a solution.

So whilst I think it's harder for a builder, I also think it's easier because you get a longer period of time— builders and landscapers— to build up that emotional connection with your client because it takes such a long time for you to put a quote together. So then it's just backing yourself, and you guys have to now.
You've got a team to support as well as your family.

You have to honor that, bottom line. Um, there we go. Brad's a bit the same. I can't believe the price I come up with sometimes, but we also know that they haven't been charging enough. Takes a lot to begin to believe in yourself to charge appropriately, but the numbers don't lie. They're not feelings, they're facts. We've got to listen to those ones. Too often we need, uh, the work, which limits the amount of profit we make.

Yes, Greg. I think that it needs to be acknowledged just how challenging it is in New Zealand and continues to be. The good news for you is you're going to have the uptake again just as we're having the, the fall, so you're going to get to remind all of us that yes, it's very tough, but we get through the other side.

So I don't think this is a lack of work from you and Shirley trying to find the work. I feel like that your industry currently is really dictating your current results and there's not a lot you can do to change that. That you haven't already done.
And we will get to the other side where it begins to lift again and you get to ride back out. It's staying in the middle that's going to be the tricky bit.
Tom and Ash, um, since moving here we've had a couple of sad cases where you just want to help the person doing too much for what they can afford. Yeah, I totally agree. I think we all get that.

This is the, the advantage and the disadvantage of working with tradies is you're the kindest people I've ever met in my life and you all want to help other people, and that usually comes at the expense of yourself.

You hear me frequently, particularly this year, I've been talking about a lot about, um, values and personal values and morals have no place in business, and that every decision you make in business needs to be made with the business in mind because it's, it's its own entity.

It feels like an extension of you because it's how it started, but as a business owner, we want you to treat it as its own entity. Therefore, the business decisions that you make for your business need to be made for the business, not from your heart. That can be really challenging at times.

It doesn't feel very comfortable. Sometimes we have to say no or stick by a price that we know somebody can't afford and you'd really like to help them out. But it's Josh, don't touch your phone while you're driving. It's either, um, your problem, you take it on, you lose money, or we let it go. Gotta honor yourself.

Um, Josh, okay, that's a sales process problem that, well, there's a sales process solution there, not so much a problem. So Josh is just saying he's got no issue with his hourly price, but if something goes wrong, the job takes longer than expected, I worry about charging too much based off the perceived value.
You did very well to type all of that while you were driving, by the way.

Um, I totally understand that it can be really, really hard, and I think that there are some steps you can place into any sales process, even a short one, Josh, for an emergency call out, that talks to some of those realities. Because they are realities.

Like, being a builder, we, we might factor in a PC or a PES sum for an area that we just can't see. Okay, I can't tell you how many times working on Queenslanders I've taken sheets off the wall and there's there's no wall, it's all termite damage, and you can't see that until you pull it off.

And that's not my responsibility to bear, it is the client's. I understand for the client it's a surprise, but if we've done the work in the sales process to preface that, they understand it's a risk.

And therefore, where I'm coming to them, sure, they're going to be disappointed and it might push them into an area that they're uncomfortable, but I've also done some work with them to advise them to have a 20% buffer because you just don't know what contingencies you'll have to deal with.
Your clients are a little bit the same.

I think that you can add a step into your process or a paragraph into an email or somewhere on your website or in your job form that maybe they fill out before they come through to you. There'll be somewhere we can add in a bit of information to preface that.
Yeah, thank you.
Josh, I don't have you. You sound like a robot. Explaining it to them, it's just overcoming— seems like a simple job, it should only take— oh, I lost you. No, I've got you.

I think you're asking how to explain it to them when they're saying things like, it seems like a simple job, it should only take a small period of time. That's okay, Josh, if you can still hear me, and it is being recorded. Thank you, Tara. Um, I think that's the information that goes out during your sales process.

It turns up in blogs on your website. It turns up in your social media, letting everybody know that sometimes what looks like the simplest job actually turns into one of the longest because of X, Y, Z. And you can give some pretty clever examples when you're talking about a blog post or a social media post.

Um, so that you can preface it like you're not— it's about transparency, I guess. And if you're doing it beforehand with transparency, even in terms and conditions, it could list it in there. The quoted time frames we give you for a job might blow out under these circumstances.

You could verbalize it when you're talking to them, or you could— the other thing that I've seen some plumbers and electricians where they work on those emergency cases, and it's not necessarily quoted works, you can preface it by saying, look, I'll, I'll do the investigation work up to this point, and then we'll discuss if I find something outside of the realms of an easy fix at that point in time, and then come back to them, have a discussion.
You can give them, you know, I think it's going to take 5 hours, but it could take 7 to 8.

If it takes 5, this is what it costs. If we go the other extreme, this is what it'll cost. So you could kind of have some breaks in that process where you discuss with them along the way. Cool. Oh, Asha, Okay, I need a show of hands. Has anybody else in the community gone through that process where you feel like being at your desk or being in the office isn't real work? You've got to be on the tools to be doing real work. I think we all have. We all feel the same.

It's kind of built into you from your apprenticeship because you got from school, and at school, yeah, Dan's saying, oh hell yeah, at school we're taught that work is sitting at your desk and doing the things, right? So we get taught that at school, that's great.
But we forget that when we go into our apprenticeship and our boss is yelling at us to work harder and faster, that we're meant to be, you know, if you're not producing, then you're not producing.

You know, if you're not doing the job on the tools, you're not producing money for the business, you're not creating the income. And so it gets drilled into you that you've got to run, run, run, work really, really, really hard, and you've got to be on the tools all the time.

When in actual fact, if you're not putting the work in front of your team members, you're not organizing the materials that are coming up, you're not continuing to navigate clients and ensure that we've got work coming through, or building on the systems and processes for your business, you have no work for them.

You've got nothing whatsoever. So when you look at it in that context, the work that you're doing in the office is actually the most important work because you're putting it in front of the team that you have to create the profitability that your family needs.
Now, that's not going to stop that little voice in your head when you sit down at the desk and you feel like that you're not achieving enough.

But I think when you, you keep the longevity, so when you look at your workflow over the next couple of weeks and you know it's all organized, if you tell yourself enough, that can be a trigger point of understanding, okay, so my job today was this.

The other problem we have with tradies, of course, is you get used to, um, a completed day of work looking like a new wall being built or a new bathroom being fitted off, or I don't know, um, um, several tidy gardens. I'm trying to think of everybody on the call, or a staircase half done.

And that forms for us what work represents. Whereas when we sit in the office, nothing changes. Absolutely nothing changes. I get to the end of my day and there's paperwork still on my desk. Nothing feels any different.

So I think you have to introduce some, almost like some KPIs for yourself so that you can look and think, okay, no, I achieved this today. So if you— it might be that your calendar's booked out for the next week and everybody knows where they're going, or it might be that your materials are coming for all of the jobs.

There'll be like little benchmarks that you can introduce into every day that you can say, okay, well, I achieved that today, rather than getting to the end of the day and thinking, looking around on your desk and thinking, well, nothing's changed, it doesn't feel any different.

Yep. Another thing I find weird too, which everyone might find this normal, is, um, because as builders, we're working like 6 or 8 or 10 weeks ahead, which is probably plenty of time when we're ordering everything. It feels like I could take today off or this whole week off and nothing would change.

So it's like you come from being on the tools where you got plasterers rocking up next week, we've got to get the frame done, to this weird, like, like you're still organizing as much as you can every day. But it doesn't really matter.

And I don't know whether it's just because we just started and the dead— and it's not, um, like we're going to start our third house in the next 3 weeks. So we'll have 3 on the go at once. Maybe it'll get busier then.
I don't know. But yeah. Okay. Maybe, maybe that feeling will go. I just enjoy it while it lasts.

I think you should enjoy it while it lasts. I think while you've got the team at the size that you currently have, you are going to have, or you have the potential for some space. So I don't think you should be upset about that, that, that potential for space is where you can be working on.

The structure so that you can actually start to bring more team in so you can be running the 3 jobs concurrently all of the time. Yeah. But it also provides you the opportunity to be networking, finding new clients, keep stacking up the work. And some of that is never going to feel like work. Yeah.

Like it doesn't— going to a networking meeting, catching up with a bunch of people over a beer or a cup of coffee never feels like work. It feels like a bit of fun, like a social activity. But that's still work. Those kind of— yeah, yeah.

Going to site and doing a bunch of photography or filming for the day doesn't feel like work, but still work because it's creating an outcome for you. So I think, um, understanding what your benchmarks are for the day, what it is that you want to achieve each day.
There's a really clever tool on the member site called the Daily Focus Journal.

Yep, the Daily Focus Journal has a morning exercise and an afternoon exercise The morning exercise is to get you clear so that you understand what you've got to achieve for the day, and the afternoon exercise gives you the reflection back over the day so you can acknowledge what you have achieved but also set your intention moving forward.

Or catch— it's a bit of a real business owner tool. It'll help you catch, um, those little red flags that we don't often see because we're too busy doing the thing. It's a very clever tool to help you with that feeling, that sense of accomplishment. Yeah. Yep. I would give that a go. Yep. Um, Dan's made an exceptional point.

The more space you can give yourself, the better you will be at decision-making and following your chosen direction. Yeah. Nice. We all need the space. It just feels uncomfortable in the beginning when we take it.

It does. And I need to change myself, change my thought pattern because I can, I've got time now to work on the business, like all the, all the stuff you've been teaching us actually. I need to get back in and make it a priority.
So I think that when you, when you set a cadence and you have a goal or an aim each day, it really changes the way you feel about the day at the end. Yep, you need some on the tools time.

Yeah, last week I went and I actually set out some houses and it was good, but most of the time I'm organizing and it feels great. We put that second tradie on And yeah, it's— it couldn't be more fun at the moment, so it's great.

I'm so pleased to hear that. I think it's essential for all tradies to have some on-the-tools time, and you do that around what works for you. That looks very different for everybody. I have— I'm yet to meet a tradie that should not spend some time on the tools.

Yeah, there's such a sense of satisfaction that comes from it. It's a great example to set for your team. You know, there's something really special about knowing that your boss can still do the hard stuff.
Now you might do it one day a week or for half a day, and they've got to do it for 5, but you used to have to do it for 5. Yeah.

Yep. But I think that, that true sense of accomplishment is really important, and you're very— tradies are exceptional at working with their hands, and if they don't have that opportunity, you do feel quite stagnant. So sometimes it's good, but not too much. Yep. Thank you for sharing. I appreciate it. Uh, there's a few people feeling the same, so you're not alone there, Asha. Yeah, I agree with you, Brad.

You should still be doing some of the things that you want to do. Maybe not all of the time, but at least some of the time. You need to have that satisfaction. Amy's revenue has tripled since she started full-time in the business. So when she looks at it like that, the office time is extremely valuable.

Hell yeah, it is extremely valuable. Okay, another question. Where do you think you confuse being busy with being productive? And where do you think you confuse being busy with being productive? On the tools.

I agree. You still making your skateboards, Dan, or do you not get time for that anymore? You are. Good. Where do you think you confuse being busy with being productive? Yeah, Amy, I think I do at times too. Busy, busy, busy, but that doesn't mean I've actually achieved anything for the day.

I believe this is where delegation has fallen down. I think if we're busy, busy, busying, there you go, Andrew's nailed it. If we're busy, busy, busy, often it's because we haven't delegated the appropriate tasks.
Sometimes we get good at delegating what feels easy to delegate, but it's not necessarily the task that will move the needle the best and creates that busyness for us. Um, so Andrew's nailed it.

And when you think back to delegating versus abdicating, it's amazing how all these themes kind of roll around together and you're all going through very similar at the same time. Okay, if your team described— okay, if you don't have a team now, think about when you have had a team.

If you do have a team, um, currently, you can think about now. If your team described your leadership style honestly, what would they say when you're under pressure? Good question. I love watching all your body language when I ask these questions.
Sometimes you get really uncomfortable and your body language completely changes, and I just love it.

Means I've asked the right question for you today. If your team described your leadership style honestly, what would they say when you're under pressure? I am exactly the same, Dan. I get very messy. That'd be the word I would use for myself. Chaos for Brad. Yep.

I get, I can't concentrate enough to delegate effectively and I just get messy and I think I make their lives a lot harder at that point. So we do a lot of work together to ensure I reach it, um, as rarely as possible.

Ash is like a cranky mom, snappy. I don't know anyone that isn't when they're feeling super pressured. Had a great session with one of our, uh, master's clients this morning about the same thing, essentially. For Amy, absent and chaotic, very reactive. Yeah, great word, Amy. Very reactive. I think we get very reactive. The other thing that can happen is people just shut down.

There's kind of two camps here. We either go into mess, chaos, barking orders, you know, that stress feels yucky, blah blah blah, or we go into complete shutdown mode because we just can't cope with all that's going on around us. Neither are great. Neither is easier to handle than the other.

They're both very challenging for the people around you. So we— it's great to be able to identify, um, either what your personal triggers are or have somebody else within your community, whether it be your partner or one of your team members, be able to let you know when you're about to approach that point.
So I've spoken many times about Adam. I can tell his triggers, they're like the easiest thing for me to read now.

I didn't always know what they were though, but these days I can see when he stops making his lunch to take with him for the day. It seems like such a little thing, but for him it's that the pressure is getting too much. He can't think with clarity in the morning to be able to create something to take for the day.
I know he's nearing proper overwhelm at that point. That's kind of the first thing that goes. The second thing that goes is that jobs blow out everywhere for him.

So when he's scheduling the team, he's putting the wrong team on jobs typically, or he's not allowing enough time for their jobs, and so they're blowing out, which is just— it's like a self-fulfilling, um, prophecy of pressure. Just keeps creating more pressure, and then he goes into shutdown.

So I can see those things happening now, and I can raise awareness around them for him, and then he can make changes. 'Or we can work together to support it. ' So you'll have significant others in your life that will see those triggers in you as well.

You know, there'll be those moments of, 'Oh, I see what's happening here. I know what's about to go on. ' And if you're open to them making you aware, you can change your behavior. That may not be something that anybody's aware of just yet.

It might be something that you need to be mindful of over the next 3, 6 months so that you can start to call each other out in that space. But definitely makes a big difference if you know what's coming. I think we all turn into our parents when the pressure's there.

I think that's a very normal reaction indeed. Okay. What part of the business do you avoid looking at because it makes you uncomfortable? I've got a couple of suggestions for you. It might be profit margins. Your business debt, your performance issues, or your own calendar, communication, clients. Yep, yep. Feels uncomfortable, you don't want to touch it. Safety stuff.

Eloise, just call, uh, what are they called? I'll remember and send you a message. Oh, NHS compliance. No, there's a better one for builders, hazard codes. I agree. Yeah, oh yeah, because the one, it's all online. You barely have to do a thing. It'll do it for you. That way you don't have to look at it.

Somebody else will look at it for you. Numbers as a whole for Brad, totally understand. Money, money's the most common thing we all ignore. It feels uncomfortable. We don't like what it says. We don't want to look at it because then we're going to understand where we're at. Too much L in the P&L. Greg, that's a great way to term it.

I like it, but I don't like it that way for you at the moment. Uh, for Dan, some of the workshop organizing, shelves full of random stuff. Oh my God, we've had to make a rule to do this twice a year, Easter and Christmas. Whole shed gets taken out, cleaned out, put back together, otherwise it turns into a shed fight.

Yes, Bright HR is different, Eloise. I don't know that you need Bright HR. We can definitely talk about what to do with that stuff, but I think for you guys Hazico is all your safety stuff. Bright HR, I think they might have an element of safety stuff, but they do more of the, the HR element of it.

So, um, the people side of it, although safety is also people side, there's just differences. And with Hazico, it's based around builders, not so much subcontractors, so make it super easy and it's all app-based. It's very, very simple. Um, client communication and quote scheduling for Amy.

Yeah, these are all really, really common. Uh, okay. If nothing changes in the next 12 months, will you be proud of the direction that you are heading? If nothing changes in the next 12 months, would you be proud of the direction you are heading? Ash and Eloise are a yes. Tom and Ash are no.

That's okay, that's what you're here for. I mean, you'll be tapped out, so she's a no. No, for Dylan. That's why you're all here. Nope. You're all making those changes now. Yes, Andrew, they'll forever be evolving. You'll just get them right and then the business will shift direction slightly, bring in another team member or expand to do something different and suddenly all your systems need reworking.

They never, ever, ever, ever end. Uh, okay, final question then. I've just got my one submitted question. We'll close out for today. Are you building this business for freedom, or have you quietly built yourself another job? I'll ask it another way.

Could you take, take a month off and your business wouldn't fall apart? If you could take a month off and your business wouldn't fall apart, you're on the way to freedom. If you can't, you're not there yet. Yes, Dan, I think you could.

Yeah, Tom and Ash, you're in, in the position you're in, you could take a month off. However, it's going to show up in other ways. Definitely a job for Brad. I mean, it's pretty typical. Maybe I should explain that we kind of have to build ourselves a job before we can effectively delegate it to somebody else.

We've got to do the do before we can hand it on. I think that, um, one of the, the problems with social media is that there are a bunch of people talking about their arrival at the perfect lifestyle, and they don't necessarily talk about how they got there.

So when you see them off living the, the entrepreneurial Bali lifestyle, it looks like it's really easy and anybody can have it, but they don't talk about the years and years and years of work or the backing from somebody else that they've received to get them there.

It takes time and work for you to create the roles in your business for you to be able to hand off to build that freedom. But that's what you're all doing here. And the financial freedom, well, it comes with delegation, but also comes with knowing your numbers, which is another reason you're all here.
Yeah, I think you're absolutely right, Asher and Eloise. I think you're on the way, Andrew. I don't think you're too far away. We're all building. All right, I had one submitted question.
Why is it that it's the newbies that are always really good at putting in the questions and our longer-term members are like, oh, I don't need a question, I'll just come along and listen?

I need your questions, gang, otherwise I've got to make these all up and then it makes you work really hard and you've got to sit there and think on a Wednesday when you probably just want to come and get your questions answered. Um, we have a very good question from Dylan.

I'm going to let our— I'm going to let you all answer this for me first and foremost, and then we'll unpack it a little bit. Dylan's question is, should he be rewarding himself for working on a Saturday or overtime like he would a normal employee? What do you think, gang?

Give me a thumbs up if you think he should be rewarding himself for working overtime. Thank you, Eloise. Thank you, Josh. Amy, absolutely, you should be rewarding yourself. You should be rewarding yourself, um, and how you reward yourself will look very different for everybody.

Is it if there's profitability in the company already? You can certainly reward yourself with a bit of extra in your pay every week if you're a company structure. That's easy to do. You can reward yourself by knowing that you're working towards a financial goal.

So you might be banking profit, or you might be working towards having 3 months of operating expenses, so you've got that backup or your safety net there. Or you might reward yourself with some time. So I regularly reward myself with some time when I travel. It normally means that I take out my Saturday as well.
So I'll travel for a Friday event with you all, and I'll have to— not have to, choose to lose my Saturday rather than try and fly home on the Friday night.

Which means I'll take the Monday off. Sometimes I'll take the Monday and Tuesday off. That's my reward for pushing hard. After conference, I take a week off. This year I'll take two. That's my reward for working really hard. I think that there needs to be some reward, but the reward will look very different based on the stage your business is at. So I would advocate for all of you to be working towards profitability goals.

To be having, um, some operating expenses put aside so that you've got that safety net behind you. And then when you reach all of those, then you can start to financially reward yourself. Now, I'll caveat this by talking about there being— we want to pay the least.
Well, there are some reasons to pay tax and reasons not to pay tax, and so you should be working with your accountant to decide what your threshold for that is.

And if you're outside of that threshold, there may be other ways to reward yourself, like more super, or perhaps there's a business investment that you can make that's going to drive your profitability moving forward. So lots of different ways to reward yourself as a business owner.
It should not all be grind, should it, Josh? We don't need to grind the whole time because Monday's Toolbox Talk and private podcast is actually all about this. It's the cadence of rest and work.

If we do the grind all the time, we start to resent our businesses and we create more stress for ourselves, and then we don't want to do it. And that's the point at which most tradies give up. So we don't ever want to do that. There needs to be a balance.
And yes, there is a time in our businesses where we need to push, but there needs to be a time where we need to pull back as well.

And so your bonus for you, Dylan, could be time, it could be financial, it could be knowing that you're saving towards a goal and that extra time that you put in actually contributes to that. It will look very different for every business, but you gotta feel like it's all worthwhile.
Um, yes, Amy Jones, lots of realigning who's doing what.

There's a couple of you working on that at the moment. I think it's good to have clear responsibility lists. Position descriptions can feel a bit stuffy and formal. If you've got a checklist of who's responsible for what, I'll take that as a position description.

It'll be enough for everybody to be clear about who's responsible for what and when, and we can then go on to describe what done looks like so there's no discrepancies. You know, my version of done on many things is very different to Adam's version of done, which creates stress that's unnecessary.

It's far easier to talk about it before you take the task on. All right, do we have any other questions come up today before we close out?
